priority_areas_checked: stored XSS via malicious link text

verdicts.title: REST Endpoint Permission Callbacks — False Positives
verdicts.analysis: All three REST endpoints properly define permission_callback. The scan controller uses get_scan_permissions() -> current_user_can('manage_options'), avatars uses get_avatar_permissions() -> current_user_can('manage_options'), and settings uses get_settings_permissions()/save_settings_permissions() -> current_user_can('manage_options'). The automated scanner's claim of missing permission_callback is factually incorrect for this version of the plugin.

verdicts.title: Second-Order SQL — any-post.php Unparameterized Query with DB-Sourced IDs
verdicts.analysis: The pattern at line 167 builds a raw SQL IN clause by imploding $current_instance_ids without prepare(). However, $current_instance_ids is sourced from wp_list_pluck() on a previous parameterized SELECT result — these are integer IDs from the blc_instances table, not user-supplied strings. The data would need to be pre-poisoned in the database. Additionally, the function is hooked to delete_post and trashed_post WordPress actions, which require delete_posts capability (Editor or higher). No unauthenticated attack path exists. The vulnerability class is theoretically a second-order injection but practically non-exploitable.

verdicts.title: Hub Endpoints (edit-link, unlink-link, nofollow-link) — WPMUDEV Dashboard Authentication
verdicts.analysis: These endpoints are registered via the wdp_register_hub_action filter, which belongs to the WPMUDEV Dashboard plugin. The Hub authentication uses HMAC-signed server-to-server requests from wpmudev.com; they cannot be triggered by arbitrary unauthenticated web requests. The XSS/RCE false-positives in the taint analysis are due to the analyzer not understanding the Hub authentication mechanism. The endpoints perform content modifications (edit/unlink/nofollow links in post content), which would be high-impact if Hub auth were bypassed, but that is outside this plugin's scope.

verdicts.title: Unauthenticated Webhook — User Review Endpoint (State Mutation Without Auth)
verdicts.analysis: The user-review webhook at the URL slug 'broken-link-checker-review/user-review' is fully public (no is_user_logged_in, no nonce check). Any visitor who supplies a valid ?token= can trigger: (1) Recipients::flag_recipient_reviewed($user_id) which permanently sets wpmudev_blc_reviewed user meta and a site-wide option, suppressing the plugin review nag. The redirect target is hardcoded to wordpress.org/support/... so there is no open redirect. Impact is limited to suppressing a review prompt. Token is computed as base64(md5(email) . '_' . key) — not easily guessable without knowing the key. NOT a critical vulnerability.
exploit_steps: 1. Obtain or guess a recipient's activation token (token = base64_encode(md5(email) . '_' . recipient_key))
3. Plugin marks the user as having reviewed, suppresses review nag permanently
verdicts.impact: Suppresses plugin review prompt; trivial state mutation

verdicts.title: Unauthenticated Webhook — Recipient Activation/Cancellation
verdicts.analysis: Two webhook actions (activate and cancel) are registered at 'broken-link-checker-activation/blc-activate-recipient' with no authentication requirement. With a valid activation_code, an unauthenticated attacker can: (1) activate an email recipient in the broken link scan notification schedule, or (2) cancel/remove an email recipient, disrupting notifications. Token = base64(md5(email) . '_' . key). For registered WordPress users, the key contains 32 random bytes from bin2hex(random_bytes(32)), making brute-force infeasible. For externally added email recipients, key entropy depends on what the admin UI generates. The main real-world risk is an attacker who intercepts an activation email performing a cancel action to unsubscribe an admin from broken-link notifications.
exploit_steps: 1. Intercept or guess a recipient's activation_code token
3. Email recipient is removed from broken-link scan notification schedule without any admin action
verdicts.impact: Unsubscribe admins from broken-link notifications; add unauthorized email recipients

verdicts.title: XSS in Email Scan Report — Email-Only Context
verdicts.analysis: The echo $origin_post_edit_url and echo $origin_post_title at lines 150/151 are inside the email view template, rendered when generating an HTML email. This is not rendered in a web browser response to a user — it is the body of an outgoing email. $origin_post_edit_url passes through admin_url() which returns a safe admin URL. $origin_post_title comes from get_the_title() which can contain HTML but is an email body, not a web page. No web XSS vector exists here.

verdicts.title: XSS in Legacy Admin Pages — Authenticated Admin-Only
verdicts.analysis: Multiple echo statements in admin pages output values without esc_html(). These are all inside WordPress admin pages requiring manage_options capability. As admin-to-admin XSS, WordPress's security model generally considers this acceptable (admins can already install plugins). These are code quality issues rather than high-severity vulnerabilities. The filter_id echo at line 1973 from $_GET['filter_id'] is a reflected XSS in the admin, but requires an admin to be tricked into clicking a crafted link — low severity in WordPress context.
verdicts.impact: Admin-context XSS only; no privilege escalation

verdicts.title: SSRF via Broken Link Checking — No Unauthenticated Trigger Found
verdicts.analysis: The primary attack surface concern was whether an unauthenticated user could trigger the URL fetching mechanism (broken link checks). Investigation of REST endpoints (all require manage_options), cron jobs (no unauthenticated schedule manipulation found), and webhooks shows no unauthenticated path to trigger URL fetching. The plugin fetches URLs only via authenticated admin actions or server-side cron triggered by WordPress's authenticated admin-initiated scheduling. No SSRF via unauthenticated path confirmed.

summary.most_critical_finding: BLC-005 — Unauthenticated webhook allows cancelling admin email notifications for broken link scanning (requires token interception)
summary.notes: No unauthenticated SSRF, SQL injection, or RCE found. Two unauthenticated webhook endpoints exist but have low impact (notification suppression/manipulation). All REST API endpoints and hub endpoints are properly authenticated. The plugin's new (app/) architecture has good security posture; the legacy/ code has admin-only XSS issues that are low-priority.
